pattern: With so few courses that the whole 'which courses are done' state fits in a bitmask, the minimum number of semesters becomes a shortest-path search over completed-course subsets, trying every legal batch of at most k newly-unlockable courses at each step.
hints: Since n is tiny, represent the set of already-completed courses as a bitmask and think of the problem as finding the fewest steps to go from the empty set to the full set.
From any completed-set state, a course is takeable this semester only if it isn't done yet and every one of its prerequisite bits is already set in that state.

Because which specific courses you pick this semester (not just how many) changes what unlocks next, you have to try every subset of the currently takeable courses of size at most k and keep the best result for each resulting completed-set state.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, four semesters needed with the same cap
maximum courses, no relations, but the cap forces one at a time
no prerequisites and the cap is large enough to finish everything at once
a strict chain forces one course per semester no matter how large the cap is
a course converges on two independent branches, so it can only start once both finish
a single course gates a branching and re-converging chain further down

stress.description: random acyclic prerequisite graph (edges only run from a lower-labeled course to a higher one, guaranteeing solvability) across the full course-count and cap range
stress.note: edges always point from a smaller label to a larger one so the graph is guaranteed acyclic and every course stays reachable

complexity.time: O(3^n) worst case over all mask/submask pairs
